title: Necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables [PDF]

paragraphs.text: ## **Necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables**

## **Jakob Runge**

German Aerospace Center Institute of Data Science 07745 Jena, Germany and Technische Universität Berlin 10623 Berlin, Germany `jakob.runge@dlr.de`

## **Abstract**

The problem of selecting optimal backdoor adjustment sets to estimate causal effects in graphical models with hidden and conditioned variables is addressed. Previous work has defined optimality as achieving the smallest asymptotic estimation variance and derived an optimal set for the case without hidden variables. For the case with hidden variables there can be settings where no optimal set exists and currently only a sufficient graphical optimality criterion of limited applicability has been derived. In the present work optimality is characterized as maximizing a certain adjustment information which allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set and a definition and algorithm to construct it. Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [Journal of Machine Learning Research, 18: 1–62, 2018] for any graph. The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation. Numerical experiments indicate that the asymptotic results also hold for relatively small sample sizes and that the optimal adjustment set or minimized variants thereof often yield better variance also beyond that estimator class. Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.

## **1 Introduction**

A standard problem setting in causal inference is to estimate the causal effect between two variables given a causal graphical model that specifies qualitative causal relations among observed variables [Pearl, 2009], including a possible presence of hidden confounding variables. The graphical model then allows to employ graphical criteria to identify valid adjustment sets, the most well-known being the _backdoor criterion_ [Pearl, 1993] and the _generalized adjustment criterion_ [Shpitser et al., 2010, Perkovi´c et al., 2015, 2018], providing a complete identification of all valid adjustment sets. Estimators of causal effects based on such a valid adjustment set as a covariate are then unbiased, but for different adjustment sets the estimation variance may strongly vary. An _optimal adjustment set_ may be characterized as one that has minimal asymptotic estimation variance. In **current work** , following Kuroki and Cai [2004] and Kuroki and Miyakawa [2003], Henckel et al. [2019] (abbreviated

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

HPM19 in the following) showed that graphical optimality always holds for linear models in the causally sufficient case where all relevant variables are observed. In Witte et al. [2020] an alternative characterization of the optimal adjustment set is discussed and the approach was integrated into the IDA algorithm [Maathuis et al., 2009, 2010] that does not require the causal graph to be known. Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019] extended the results in HPM19 to asymptotically linear non-parametric graphical models. HPM19’s optimal adjustment set holds for the causally sufficient case (no hidden variables) and the authors gave an example with hidden variables where optimality does not hold in general, i.e., the optimal adjustment set depends on the coefficients and noise terms (more generally, the distribution), rather than just the graph. Most recently, Smucler et al. [2021] (SSR20) partially extended these results to the non-parametric hidden variables case together with _dynamic treatment regimes_ , i.e., conditional causal effects. SSR20 provide a sufficient criterion for an optimal set to exist and a definition based on a certain undirected graph-construction using a result by van der Zander et al. [2019]. However, their sufficient criterion is very restrictive and a current major open problem is a _necessary_ and sufficient condition for an optimal adjustment set to exist in the hidden variable case and a corresponding definition of an optimal set.

My **main theoretical contribution** is a solution to this problem. Optimality for conditional causal effects in the hidden variables case is fully characterized by an information-theoretic approach involving a certain difference of conditional mutual informations among the observed variables termed the adjustment information. Maximizing the adjustment information formalizes the common intuition to choose adjustment sets that maximally constrain the effect variable and minimally constrain the cause variable. This allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set. The derived optimal adjustment set also has the property of minimum cardinality, i.e., no node can be removed without sacrificing optimality. Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not. The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation that, at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case. As **practical contributions** the paper provides extensive numerical experiments that corroborate the theoretical results and show that the optimal adjustment set or minimized variants thereof often yield better variance also beyond the theoretically analyzed estimator class. Code is available in the python package `https://github.com/jakobrunge/tigramite` . More detailed preliminaries, proofs, algorithms, and further numerical experiments are given in the Supplementary Material.

## **1.1 Preliminaries and problem setting**

We consider causal effects in causal graphical models over a set of variables **V** with a joint distribution _P_ = _P_ ( **V** ) that is consistent with an acyclic directed mixed graph (ADMG) _G_ = ( **V** _, E_ ). Two nodes can have possibly more than one edge which can be _directed_ ( _←_ ) or _bi-directed_ ( _↔_ ). See Fig. 1A for an example. Kinships are defined as usual: parents _pa_ ( _X_ ) for “ _•→X_ ”, spouses _sp_ ( _X_ ) for “ _X↔•_ ”, children _ch_ ( _X_ ) for “ _X→•_ ”. These sets all exclude _X_ . Correspondingly descendants _des_ ( _X_ ) and ancestors _an_ ( _X_ ) are defined, which, on the other hand, both include _X_ . The mediator nodes on causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are denoted **M** = **M** ( _X, Y_ ) and exclude _X_ and _Y_ . For detailed preliminaries, including the definition of open and blocked paths, see Supplementary Section A. In this work we only consider a univariate intervention variable _X_ and effect variable _Y_ . We simplify set notation and denote unions of variables as _{W } ∪_ **M** _∪_ **A** = _W_ **MA** .

A (possibly empty) set of adjustment variables **Z** for the total causal effect of _X_ on _Y_ in an ADMG is called _valid_ relative to ( _X, Y_ ) if the interventional distribution for setting _do_ ( _X_ = **x** ) [Pearl, 2009] factorizes as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = � _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** _,_ **z** ) _p_ ( **z** ) _d_ **z** for non-empty **Z** and as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** ) for empty **Z** . Valid adjustment sets, the set of which is here denoted _Z_ , can be read off from a given causal graph using the generalized adjustment criterion [Perkovi´c et al., 2015, 2018] which generalizes Pearl’s back-door criterion [Pearl, 2009]. A set **Z** is valid if both of the following conditions hold: (i) **Z** _∩_ **forb** = _∅_ , and (ii) all non-causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are blocked by **Z** . An adjustment set is called _minimal_ if no strict subset of **Z** is still valid. We assume a non-zero causal effect from X on Y , potentially through a set of mediators_ **M** _, and given selected conditioned variables_ **S** _, where_ **S** _∩_ **forb** = _∅. ## **2 Optimal adjustment sets**

## **2.1 Information-theoretic characterization**

Figure 1B illustrates two causal effect estimates for a linear Gaussian model consistent with the graph in Fig. 1A. With **Z** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 (blue) the error is much larger than with **O** = _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 (orange) for two reasons: **Z** constrains the residual variance _V ar_ ( _Y |_ **ZS** ) of the effect variable _Y_ less than **O** and, on the other hand, **Z** constrains the residual variance _V ar_ ( _X|_ **ZS** ) of the cause variable _X_ more than **O** . Smaller estimator variance also holds for **O** compared to any other valid set in _Z_ here.

We information-theoretically formalize the resulting intuition to choose an adjustment set **Z** that maximally constrains the effect variable _Y_ and minimally constrains the cause variable _X_ . In terms of CMIs and given selected fixed conditions **S** the quantity to maximize can be stated as follows.

**Definition 1** (Adjustment information) **.** _Consider a causal effect of X on Y for an adjustment set_ **Z** _given a condition set_ **S** _. The_ (conditional) adjustment (set) information _, abbreviated J_ **Z** _, is defined as_

_J_ **Z** is not necessarily positive if the dependence between _X_ and **Z** (given **S** ) is larger than that between **Z** and _Y_ given _X_ **S** . Equation (6) follows from the CMI definition. Fig. 1C illustrates the two CMIs in Eq. (5) in a Venn diagram.

Before discussing the range of estimators for which maximizing the adjustment information _J_ **Z** leads to a minimal asymptotic estimation variance in Sect. 2.2, we characterize graphical optimality in an information-theoretic framework. Our goal is to provide graphical criteria for optimal adjustment sets, i.e., criteria that depend only on the structure of the graph _G_ and not on the distribution.

**Definition 2** (Information-theoretical graphical optimality) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 we say that_ (information-theoretical) graphical optimality holds _if there is a_ **Z** _∈Z such that either there is no other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z or for all other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z and_ all _distributions P consistent with G we have J_ **Z** _≥ J_ **Z** _′._

My main result builds on the following lemma which relates graphical optimality to informationtheoretic inequalities in a necessary and sufficient comparison condition for an optimal set to exist. **Lemma 1** (Necessary and sufficient comparison criterion for existence of an optimal set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1, if and only if there is a_ **Z** _∈Z such that either there is no other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z or for all other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z and all distributions P consistent with G it holds that_

_then graphical optimality holds and_ **Z** _is optimal implying J_ **Z** _≥ J_ **Z** _′._

In SSR20 and HPM19 the corresponding conditional independence statements to the terms (iii) and (iv) in the inequalities (7) are used as a sufficient pairwise comparison criterion. However, Lemma 1 shows that for graphical optimality it is not necessary that terms (iii) and (iv) vanish, they just need to fulfill the inequalities (7) for a _necessary_ and sufficient criterion.

In principle, Lemma 1 can be used to cross-compare all pairs of sets, but firstly, it is difficult to explicitly evaluate (7) for all distributions _P_ consistent with _G_ and, secondly, iterating through all valid adjustment sets is computationally prohibitive even for small graph sizes. As an example, consider a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes. Then this path can be blocked by 2[5] _−_ 1 different subsets. In the main result of this work (Thm. 3) a necessary and sufficient criterion based purely on graphical properties is given.

## **2.3 Definition of O-set**

The optimal adjustment set for the causally sufficient case is simply **P** = _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) _\_ **forb** and was derived in HPM19 and Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019]. In Section B.2 the derivation is discussed from an information-theoretic perspective. In the case with hidden variables we need to account for bidirected edges “ _↔_ ” which considerably complicate the situation. Then the parents of _Y_ **M** are not sufficient to block all non-causal paths. Further, just like conditioning on parents of _Y_ **M** leads to optimality in the sufficient case since parents constrain information in _Y_ **M** , in the hidden variables case also conditioning on spouses of _Y_ **M** constrains information about _Y_ **M** .

**Example A.** A simple graph (ADMG) to illustrate this is _X→Y ↔Z_ 1 (shown with an additional **S** in Fig. 2A below, or Fig. 4 in SSR20). Here **Z** _[′]_ = _∅_ = **vancs** is a valid set, but it is not optimal. Consider **O** = _Z_ 1, then term (iii) = 0 in the inequalities (7) since **Z** _[′] \_ **O** = _∅_ . Even though not needed to block non-causal paths (there is none), _Z_ 1 still constrains information in _Y_ while being independent of _X_ (hence, term (iv) = 0) which leads to _J_ **O** _> J∅_ according to the inequalities (7).

Not only direct spouses can constrain information in _Y_ as Fig. 2B below illustrates. Since for _W ∈ Y_ **M** the motif “ _W ↔ C_ 1 _←∗C_ 2” (“ _∗_ ” denotes either edge mark) is open, it holds that _I_ ( _C_ 1 _C_ 2; _W_ ) = _I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )+ _I_ ( _C_ 2; _W |C_ 1) _≥ I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ ) and we can even further increase the first term in the adjustment

In the following we will abbreviate **O** = **O** ( _X, Y,_ **S** ). Algorithm C.1 states efficient pseudo-code to construct the **O** -set and detect whether a valid adjustment set exists. Since none of the conditions of Def. 3 for adding collider nodes depends on previously added nodes, the algorithm is orderindependent. The statement occurring in lines 11 and 21 (“No valid adjustment set exists.”) is proven in Thm. 1. If the graph is a DAG, then lines 4-22 can be omitted. Numerical experiments in Section 3 will show that further interesting adjustment sets are the _minimized_ **O** -set **O** min, where **O** is minimized such that no subset can be removed without making **O** min invalid, and the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** _\_ **P** _⊆_ **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid. Both adjustment sets can be constructed with Alg. C.2 similar to the efficient algorithms in van der Zander et al. [2019]. Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops. Based on the idea in **O** Cmin, in the numerical experiments we also consider AdjustXmin, where only Adjust _\ pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) is minimized and _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) is always included. Finally, we also evaluate Adjustmin where Adjust is fully minimized.

Before discussing the optimality of the **O** -set, we need to assure that it is a valid adjustment set. Similar to the proof given in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for the validity of the **vancs** -set (for the case without **S** ), we can state that the **O** -set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists.

**Theorem 1** (Validity of O-set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 but_ without _a priori assuming that a valid adjustment set exists (apart from the requirement_ **S** _∩_ **forb** = _∅). If and only if a valid backdoor adjustment set exists, then_ **O** _is a valid adjustment set._

## **2.4 Graphical optimality**

We now move to the question of optimality. It is known that there are graphs where no graphical criterion exists to determine optimality. Examples, discussed later, are the graphs in Figs. 2E,F.

Before stating necessary and sufficient conditions for graphical optimality, I mention that next to the **O** -set defined above and the Adjust set **vancs** [Perkovi´c et al., 2018], I am not aware of any other systematically constructed set that will yield a valid adjustment set for the case with hidden variables. van der Zander et al. [2019] provide algorithms to list all valid adjustment sets, but the question is which of these a user should choose. As mentioned above, Lemma 1 can be used to cross-compare all pairs of sets, but this is not really feasible. Hence, for automated causal effect estimation, rather than the question of whether graphical optimality holds, it is crucial to have a set with better properties than other systematically constructable sets. In the following the **O** -set is illustrated and conditions for graphical optimality are explored. SSR20 provide a sufficient condition for optimality, which states that either all nodes are observed (no bidirected edges exist) or for all observed nodes **V** _⊂_ **vancs** . This is a very strict assumption and not fulfilled for any of the examples (except for Example G) discussed in the following.

**Example B.** Figure 2B depicts a larger example to illustrate the **O** -set **O** = **PCPC** with **P** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 _Z_ 4 (blue boxes) and **CPC** _\_ **P** = _Z_ 5 _Z_ 6 _Z_ 7 _Z_ 8 (green boxes). We also have a conditioned variable **S** . Among **P** , only _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 are needed to block non-causal paths to _X_ , _Z_ 3 _Z_ 4 are only there to constrain information in _Y_ . Here the same holds for the whole set **CPC** _\_ **P** which was constructed from the paths _Y ↔Z_ 5 _↔Z_ 6 _←Z_ 7 and _Y ↔Z_ 5 _↔Z_ 8 which does not include _Z_ 9 since it is a descendant of _Y_ **M** . Including an independent variable like _Z_ 12 in **O** would not decrease the adjustment information _J_ **O** , but then **O** would not be of minimum cardinality anymore (proven in Cor. B.1). Here, again, the condition of SSR20 does not hold (e.g., _Z_ 5 is not an ancestor of _XY_ **S** ). **O** is optimal here which can be seen as follows: For term (iii) in the inequalities (7) to even be non-zero, we would need a valid **Z** such that **Z** _\_ **O** has a path to _Y_ given **OS** _X_ . But these are all blocked. Note that while _Z_ 10 or _Z_ 9 _∈_ **Z** would open a path to _Y_ , both of these are descendants of **M** or _Y_ and, hence, cannot be in a valid **Z** . For term (iv) to even be non-zero **O** _\_ **Z** would need to have a path to _X_ given **ZS** . But since any valid **Z** has to contain _Z_ 1 and _Z_ 2 (or _Z_ 11), the only nodes in **O** with a path to _X_ are parents of _Y_ **M** and paths from these parents to _X_ all need to be blocked for a valid **Z** . Hence, **O** is optimal here.

## **4 Discussion and Conclusions**

The proposed adjustment information formalizes the common intuition to choose adjustment sets that maximally constrain the effect variable and minimally constrain the cause variable. The main **theoretical contributions** are a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set in the hidden variables case and a definition and algorithm to construct it. To emphasize, graphical optimality implies that the **O** -set is optimal for _any distribution_ consistent with the graph. Note that in cases where graphical optimality does not hold, there will still be distributions for which the **O** -set has maximal adjustment information.

Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not. This makes the **O** -set a natural choice in automated causal inference analyses. **Practical contributions** comprise Python code to construct adjustment sets and check optimality, as well as extensive numerical experiments that demonstrate that the theoretical results also hold for relatively small sample sizes.

The theoretical **optimality results are limited** to estimators for which the asymptotic variance becomes minimal for adjustment sets with maximal adjustment information (relation (8)). This is fulfilled for least-squares estimators, where even the direct relation (9) holds, but it is unclear whether this also holds for more general classes. The numerical results show that the **O** -set or minimized variants thereof often yield smaller variance also in non-optimal settings and beyond that estimator class. I speculate that further theoretical properties of maximizing adjustment information can be shown because relation (9) for _f_ ( _·_ ) = ~~_√_~~ 1 _n[e][H][Y][ |][X]_ **[ZS]** _[−][H][X][|]_ **[ZS]**[seems related to the lower bound of the] estimation variance counterpart to Fano’s inequality (Theorem 8.6.6 in Cover and Thomas [2006]). For estimators sensitive to high-dimensionality one may consider data-driven criteria or penalties to step-wisely minimize the **O** -set. However, estimating, for example, the adjustment information from a potentially small sample size carries considerable errors itself. Another current limitation is that relation (9) only holds for univariate singleton cause variables _X_ . The information-theoretical results, however, also hold for multivariate **X** and preliminary results indicate that, while relation (9) does not hold for multivariate **X** , the less restrictive relation (8) still seems to hold.

The proposed information-theoretic approach can guide **further research** , for example, to theoretically study relations (8),(9) for other estimators and to address other types of graphs as emerge from the output of causal discovery algorithms and the setting where the graph is unknown [Witte et al., 2020, Maathuis et al., 2009, 2010]. At present, the approach only applies to ADMGs and _Maximal Ancestral Graphs_ (MAG) [Richardson and Spirtes, 2002] without selection variables. Last, it remains an open problem to identify optimal adjustment estimands for the hidden variables case based on other criteria such as the front-door formula and Pearl’s general do-calculus [Pearl, 2009].

The results may carry considerable **practical impact** since, surprisingly, among the randomly created configurations more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many realworld scenarios graphical optimality may hold. progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant theoretical advance in causal graphical models by providing a necessary and sufficient graphical condition for the existence of an optimal adjustment set in the presence of hidden variables—a longstanding open problem in the field. The core innovation lies in the introduction of *adjustment information*, an information-theoretic quantity that enables a clean characterization of optimality in terms of maximizing this measure, leading to a complete graphical criterion for when such an optimal set exists and how to construct it. The proposed framework generalizes prior work by extending beyond the causally sufficient setting and subsumes previous sufficient conditions, while also offering an algorithmic construction of the optimal set with desirable properties such as minimality in cardinality and guaranteed validity whenever any valid adjustment set exists. The clarity of exposition is high, particularly in the structured presentation of the problem context, limitations of existing approaches, and the precise statement of contributions. Nevertheless, a major issue is the lack of detailed explanation or proof sketch regarding how the information-theoretic analysis connects to asymptotic variance for the claimed class of estimators, especially beyond the linear case; while the author notes that this connection is currently only verified for linear models, the claim of broader relevance would benefit from deeper justification. Additionally, the paper assumes substantial familiarity with advanced graphical criteria (e.g., generalized adjustment, m-connectedness), which may hinder accessibility for non-specialists despite the supplementary material. Overall, the paper is technically strong, conceptually insightful, and empirically well-supported, but its impact could be enhanced by clarifying the scope and limitations of the variance optimality claims in non-linear or non-parametric regimes.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of adjustment information range
comments.quote: _J_ **Z** is not necessarily positive if the dependence between _X_ and **Z** (given **S** ) is larger than that between **Z** and _Y_ given _X_ **S** .
comments.explanation: The statement correctly notes that adjustment information $ J_\mathbf{Z} = I(\mathbf{Z}; Y | X\mathbf{S}) - I(X; \mathbf{Z} | \mathbf{S}) $ can be negative, which is mathematically accurate. However, the phrasing 'not necessarily positive' may misleadingly suggest negativity is common or expected under certain graph structures. In practice, for valid adjustment sets, $I(\mathbf{Z}; Y | X\mathbf{S})$ often dominates due to **Z**'s role in explaining **Y**. While the paper avoids claiming positivity and appropriately cautions against assuming $J_\mathbf{Z} \geq 0$, the wording could be clearer to prevent misinterpretation. The issue is minor because the statement remains technically correct.

comments.title: Potential confusion between graphical optimality and estimator-specific optimality
comments.quote: My main theoretical contribution is a solution to this problem. Optimality for conditional causal effects in the hidden variables case is fully characterized by an information-theoretic approach involving a certain difference of conditional mutual informations... This allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set.
comments.explanation: The paper claims a necessary and sufficient *graphical* criterion for optimal adjustment sets based on maximizing adjustment information $J_\mathbf{Z}$, which is distribution-dependent. This risks misinterpretation: how can a distribution-dependent quantity yield a purely graphical condition? The resolution lies in Lemma 1 and Theorem 3 (implied), where the criterion identifies when one set $\mathbf{Z}$ dominates all others *across all distributions consistent with the graph*, i.e., universal dominance. Thus, the resulting condition is indeed graphical. However, the transition from information-theoretic formulation to graphical result is not sufficiently clarified in early sections, increasing the risk that readers conflate distribution-specific optimality with structural determinacy. The argument is logically sound, but the presentation could better emphasize that graphical optimality holds only when information-theoretic dominance is guaranteed by structure alone.

comments.title: Misleading claim about equivalence of maximizing J_Z and minimizing adjustment entropy
comments.quote: Minimizing the adjustment entropy is by Eq. (6) equivalent to maximizing the adjustment information.
comments.explanation: The term 'adjustment entropy' is never formally defined. From context, it appears to refer to $H(Y|XZS) + H(X|ZS)$, interpreted as a combined cost. However, $J_\mathbf{Z} = I(\mathbf{Z}; Y | X\mathbf{S}) - I(X; \mathbf{Z} | \mathbf{S})$ translates to adjustments in *both* entropies: reducing $H(Y|XZS)$ increases $I(\mathbf{Z}; Y | X\mathbf{S})$, while increasing $H(X|ZS)$ reduces $I(X; \mathbf{Z} | \mathbf{S})$. Hence, maximizing $J_\mathbf{Z}$ involves a trade-off, not minimization of a single entropy-like quantity. The claim falsely frames the objective as unidimensional minimization. In Gaussian linear models, the asymptotic variance depends on $\log \sigma^2_{Y|XZS} - \log \sigma^2_{X|ZS}$, which *is* proportional to the entropy *difference*, not the sum. Thus, while the logic in Lemma 2 is valid under correct model assumptions, the term 'adjustment entropy' is misleading and oversimplifies a nuanced optimization trade-off.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that OLS in linear models satisfies relation (9) without Gaussian noise
comments.quote: This relation is also the basis of the results for the causally sufficient case in Henckel et al. [2019] where it is shown that it holds more generally for causal linear models that do not require the noise terms to be Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that the asymptotic variance of OLS depends only on conditional variances (and thus entropies), even for non-Gaussian errors. However, for non-Gaussian linear models, mutual information involves higher-order moments and isn't solely determined by log-variance. While under homoskedasticity, the asymptotic variance of OLS reduces to $\sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1}$ with consistent $\hat{\sigma}^2$, the precision of $\hat{\sigma}^2$ — and hence finite-sample performance — depends on kurtosis. More importantly, equating $J_\mathbf{Z}$ with estimator variance requires elliptical (e.g., Gaussian) distributions for exact equivalence. The reference to Henckel et al. [2019] likely relies on relative ordering via partial $R^2$, which *is* variance-based and may preserve optimal set selection. But the *exact* information-theoretic equivalence claimed in relation (9) does not hold generally. This overstates the theoretical generality of the result outside Gaussian assumptions.

The underlying idea is correct — paths stop at nodes outside **vancs** or d-separated from **X** — but the logical expression is flawed.

comments.title: Missing justification for order-independence of minimization procedures
comments.quote: Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops.
comments.explanation: The claim that **O**min and **O**Cmin are order-independent 'since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops' is insufficient. Post-loop removal does not guarantee uniqueness; order-independence requires monotonicity of validity or lattice structure of valid subsets. In adjustment set minimization, greedy backward elimination can yield different results depending on removal order unless the problem has special structure (e.g., antichains, meet-closure). The cited work (van der Zander et al., 2019) provides such guarantees, but no reference is made here. Given that the reliability of **O**min and **O**Cmin hinges on reproducibility, and no graph-theoretic argument supports the claim, this omission is a moderate flaw in methodological rigor.

comments.title: Incorrect logical formulation in Theorem 2 condition for equality
comments.quote: We have J_O = J_vancs only if (1) O = vancs, or (2) O ⊆ vancs and X ⊥⊥ vancs \ O | OS.
comments.explanation: The use of 'only if' makes this a claim of *necessity*: these are the only two scenarios under which $J_\mathbf{O} = J_\textbf{vancs}$. But other cases exist — for instance, if variables in $\mathbf{O} \setminus \textbf{vancs}$ are conditionally independent of both $Y$ given $X\mathbf{S}$ and $X$ given $\mathbf{S}$, their contributions to $J_\mathbf{Z}$ cancel out even if $\mathbf{O} \not\subseteq \textbf{vancs}$. The second condition unnecessarily restricts $\mathbf{O} \subseteq \textbf{vancs}$. While the stated conditions are sufficient, claiming necessity is too strong. The authors likely meant 'if' rather than 'only if'. As written, the theorem is potentially false and overreaching for a result intended to hold on any graph, making this a moderate theoretical flaw.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing in Theorem 3 condition for N-nodes
comments.quote: Denote the set of N-nodes by **N** = _sp_ ( _Y_ **MC** ) _\_ ( **forbOS** ) . Finally, given an N ∈_ **N** _and a collider path N ↔· · · ↔C↔· · · ↔W (including N ↔W ) for C ∈_ **C** _and W ∈ Y_ **M** _(indexed by i) with the collider path nodes denoted by πi[N][(excluding][ N][and][ W][), denote by]_ **[ O]** _π_ i[N][=] **[ O]**[(] _[X, Y,]_ **[ S]** _[′]_[=] **[ S]** _[Nπ] i[N]_[)] _[ the O-set for the causal effect] of X on Y given_ **S** _[′]_ = **S** _∪{N } ∪ πi[N][.
comments.explanation: The notation 'πi[N]' is ambiguous: it appears to index a path starting at node $N$, but written as $\pi_i[N]$, suggesting $N$ is an index rather than a node. Likely a typo from OCR, intended as $\pi^{(i)}(N)$ or $\pi_i(N)$. Further, $\mathbf{S}' = \mathbf{S} \cup \{N\} \cup \pi_i[N]$ defines a new conditioning set, but it's unclear whether $\pi_i[N]$ includes only internal nodes and whether all such paths or just one are considered. This affects Condition (I) in Theorem 3, which checks if $\mathbf{O}_{\pi_iN}$ blocks non-causal paths. Without specifying path enumeration, node exclusion, or union scope, the construction lacks reproducibility. Examples F and G hint at the intent, but algorithmic clarity is compromised, making verification difficult.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of O-set in text vs. algorithmic implication
comments.quote: **O** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 in Example E; Definition 4 states **O** = **P** ∪ **C** ∪ **PC**
comments.explanation: In Example E, **O** = $Z_1Z_2$. According to Definition 4, if $Z_2$ is a collider ($Z_2 \leftrightarrow Y$) and in **vancs**, then $Z_2 \in \mathbf{C}$. Then $\mathbf{PC} = pa(\mathbf{C}) = pa(Z_2)$. But in an ADMG, if $Z_1 \leftrightarrow Z_2$, $Z_1$ is not a parent of $Z_2$, so $Z_1 \notin \mathbf{PC}$ under strict interpretation. Yet the inclusion of $Z_1$ in **O** suggests $\mathbf{PC}$ includes spouses or neighbors, contradicting the definition. This implies either an OCR error or a real definitional gap: is 'pa' used in the strict DAG sense or extended to include bidirected neighbors for collider paths? The algorithm (C.1) likely resolves this, but without clarification, the definition is ambiguous and affects **PC**'s graphical interpretation.

comments.title: Missing clarification on validity of minimized O-sets
comments.quote: Numerical experiments in Section 3 will show that further interesting adjustment sets are the _minimized_ **O** -set **O** min, where **O** is minimized such that no subset can be removed without making **O** min invalid, and the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** \ **P** \u2286 **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces **O**min and **O**Cmin but does not explicitly state that minimization preserves validity: i.e., that every subset considered during removal still satisfies the adjustment criterion. Without this, a reader might assume any backward elimination suffices, risking production of an invalid set. Though Algorithm C.2 likely ensures validity, the main text should emphasize that minimization is *constrained* to valid subsets, following standard practices (e.g., van der Zander et al., 2019). As written, the method is underspecified for reproducibility, especially since removing even one collider-path node could open a non-causal path. This is a moderate technical gap.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about O-set's variance compared to Adjust-set
comments.quote: Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not.
comments.explanation: The passage asserts that the **O**-set uniformly has lower or equal variance compared to the Adjust-set across all graphs and validity conditions. However, the numerical results show that **O** underperforms **O**Cmin and AdjustXmin in about half the cases, indicating it does not universally dominate other adjustment sets in variance. While **O** may dominate Adjust in *adjustment information*, translating this to strict *variance dominance* across all graphs, estimators, and sample sizes — given **O**'s higher cardinality — is unsupported. The claim overreaches by ignoring finite-sample effects and estimator-specific sensitivities, especially since AdjustXmin sometimes outperforms **O**. The equivalence in variance relies on Assumption 2, which does not hold generally. This constitutes a moderate overstatement of the **O**-set’s superiority.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant theoretical advance in causal graphical models by providing a necessary and sufficient graphical condition for the existence of an optimal adjustment set in the presence of hidden variables—a longstanding open problem in the field. The core innovation lies in the introduction of *adjustment information*, an information-theoretic quantity that enables a clean characterization of optimality in terms of maximizing this measure, leading to a complete graphical criterion for when such an optimal set exists and how to construct it. The proposed framework generalizes prior work by extending beyond the causally sufficient setting and subsumes previous sufficient conditions, while also offering an algorithmic construction of the optimal set with desirable properties such as minimality in cardinality and guaranteed validity whenever any valid adjustment set exists. The clarity of exposition is high, particularly in the structured presentation of the problem context, limitations of existing approaches, and the precise statement of contributions. Nevertheless, a major issue is the lack of detailed explanation or proof sketch regarding how the information-theoretic analysis connects to asymptotic variance for the claimed class of estimators, especially beyond the linear case; while the author notes that this connection is currently only verified for linear models, the claim of broader relevance would benefit from deeper justification. Additionally, the paper assumes substantial familiarity with advanced graphical criteria (e.g., generalized adjustment, m-connectedness), which may hinder accessibility for non-specialists despite the supplementary material. Overall, the paper is technically strong, conceptually insightful, and empirically well-supported, but its impact could be enhanced by clarifying the scope and limitations of the variance optimality claims in non-linear or non-parametric regimes.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of adjustment information range
comments.quote: _J_ **Z** is not necessarily positive if the dependence between _X_ and **Z** (given **S** ) is larger than that between **Z** and _Y_ given _X_ **S** .
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement seems fine—it correctly notes that the adjustment information $ J_\mathbf{Z} = I(\mathbf{Z}; Y | X\mathbf{S}) - I(X; \mathbf{Z} | \mathbf{S}) $ can be negative, since the two CMIs are not necessarily ordered. The intuition given—that if $X$ and $\mathbf{Z}$ are more dependent (conditionally on $\mathbf{S}$) than $\mathbf{Z}$ and $Y$ (conditionally on $X\mathbf{S}$), then $J_\mathbf{Z}$ can be negative—is logically sound and consistent with information theory. However, the phrasing ‘not necessarily positive’ is slightly misleading because it could imply that negativity is common or expected under certain graph structures, whereas in practice for valid adjustment sets, one might expect $I(\mathbf{Z}; Y | X\mathbf{S})$ to often dominate due to the role of $\mathbf{Z}$ in explaining $Y$. That said, the concern is mitigated by the fact that the paper does not claim positivity, and the possibility of negative values is mathematically correct. The statement serves mainly to caution against assuming $J_\mathbf{Z} \geq 0$, which is appropriate. Thus, while the wording could be clearer, it's not technically incorrect.

comments.title: Potential confusion between graphical optimality and estimator-specific optimality
comments.quote: My main theoretical contribution is a solution to this problem. Optimality for conditional causal effects in the hidden variables case is fully characterized by an information-theoretic approach involving a certain difference of conditional mutual informations... This allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set.
comments.explanation: Initially, this claim appears strong: the author claims to have derived a necessary and sufficient *graphical* condition for the existence of an optimal adjustment set, which would mean that whether such a set exists—and what it is—depends only on the graph structure. This is a significant contribution. However, upon closer inspection, this characterization hinges on maximizing adjustment information $J_\mathbf{Z}$, which is distribution-dependent. So the concern arises: how can a distribution-dependent quantity lead to a purely *graphical* criterion? The resolution comes in Lemma 1 and Theorem 3 (implied but not shown), where the key is that graphical optimality holds if one set $\mathbf{Z}$ dominates all others *for all distributions consistent with the graph*, i.e., $J_\mathbf{Z} \geq J_{\mathbf{Z}'}$ universally. The paper later clarifies that the proposed condition identifies when such universal dominance occurs, which is indeed a graphical property. So the claim is valid, but the transition from an information-theoretic quantity to a graphical criterion could be more explicitly justified in the introduction to prevent misreading. The argument is logically sound, but the risk is that readers might conflate 'optimality via information' with 'automatic graphical determination', whereas the paper correctly identifies the subset of graphs where information-theoretic dominance is guaranteed by structure alone.

comments.title: Inconsistency in notation for conditioning in CMI terms
comments.quote: _IX_; _Y |Z_ = _HY |Z − HY |ZX_
comments.explanation: The notation used here for conditional mutual information is $I(X; Y | Z) = H(Y | Z) - H(Y | ZX)$, which is standard. However, in the expression $HY |ZX$, the order and grouping of variables might raise a red flag: should it be $H(Y | Z, X)$ or $H(Y | XZ)$? In the text, it's written as $HY |ZX$, which, under the author’s earlier notation ('{W } ∪ **M** ∪ **A** = W MA'), implies concatenation without commas. This is consistent with the simplifying convention introduced: $\mathbf{Z}X = \mathbf{Z} \cup \{X\}$. So $H(Y | ZX)$ is a shorthand for $H(Y | \mathbf{Z}, X)$, which is correct. Thus, $I(X; Y | \mathbf{Z}) = H(Y | \mathbf{Z}) - H(Y | \mathbf{Z}, X)$ is correctly represented. Although this could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the notation, it is internally consistent with the earlier simplification rule. No actual error—but the notation $ZX$ (instead of $X\mathbf{Z}$) slightly breaks the pattern: elsewhere, $X\mathbf{S}$ is used, not $\mathbf{S}X$. Still, since union is commutative, it's harmless. Given that the author later uses $X\mathbf{S}$ consistently, this may be a minor typographical inconsistency, possibly from OCR, but not semantically problematic.

comments.title: Missing qualification in estimator class applicability
comments.quote: The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation. [...] at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case.
comments.explanation: The author correctly states that the information-theoretic optimality (via maximizing $J_\mathbf{Z}$) translates to minimal asymptotic variance only for estimators satisfying a certain relation (Eq. 9), and acknowledges that this has only been verified for the linear case. However, the abstract and introduction present the results as broadly applicable, e.g., 'numerical experiments [...] show that the optimal adjustment set [...] often yield better variance also beyond that estimator class'. While this empirical finding is valuable, the initial claim that the 'results translate' might be misinterpreted as a proven theoretical result beyond linear models. The concern is that without a theoretical guarantee, the 'translation' is currently only conjectural outside the linear setting. But the paper does include sufficient caveats: it says 'for a class of estimators', and limits theoretical verification to linear models. So the claim is not overstated—rather, the emphasis on the empirical success in experiments (as supported in supplementary material) justifies the broader optimism. Still, a careful reader might question how much of the 'solution to the problem' relies on unproven generalization. Given the honesty in limiting theoretical claims, the issue is minor but highlights a tension between theoretical scope and empirical promise.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of O-set components
comments.quote: **Definition 4** (O-set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 and the definition of valid colliders in Def. 3, define the set_ **O** ( _X, Y,_ **S** ) = **P** _∪_ **C** _∪_ **PC** _where_
comments.explanation: The definition is cut off in the provided passage—only the beginning of Definition 4 is given, with no specification of what **P**, **C**, and **PC** are. This makes it impossible to verify correctness. However, based on context and standard naming: in Section 2.3, **P** was introduced as $pa(Y\mathbf{M}) \setminus \textbf{forb}$, **C** likely denotes valid collider path nodes of $Y\mathbf{M}$, and **PC** their parents. But since the full definition is missing in the passage, a reader cannot confirm whether the construction is precise. In particular, it's critical whether **C** includes only valid collider path nodes from Def. 3 (which requires conditions involving **vancs** and independence from $X$), and whether **PC** includes parents only of those nodes. The truncation here is due to the input boundary, not the paper itself. Since the instruction says not to flag incomplete text at passage boundaries, and since the definition likely continues in the full paper, this is not a real issue. But if this were a standalone excerpt, it would be severely underspecified.

comments.title: Incomplete definition of 'forb' set due to OCR truncation
comments.quote: (henceforth just denoted as **forb** ). A set **Z** is valid if both of the following conditions hold: (i) **Z** _∩_ **forb** = _∅_ , and (ii) all non-causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are blocked by **Z** .
comments.explanation: The passage defines the validity of an adjustment set in terms of **forb**, but the actual definition of **forb** is missing—it appears that the mathematical expression was truncated or lost due to OCR. In the context, **forb** is standardly known as the 'forbidden set', typically defined as _forb(X, Y) = de(Y)_ (descendants of Y) in the presence of mediators, possibly excluding _X_ and _Y_. The paper summary later confirms that **forb** contains descendants of _Y_**M**, and the example mentions _Z_4 ∈ **forb** because it is a descendant of _Y_**M**. So despite the missing formula, the intended meaning can be reconstructed from context. However, in an academic paper, the explicit definition should not be omitted even if standard—especially since the paper modifies notation with mediators. This is likely an OCR error where the formula was not captured. The concept is correctly used later (e.g., in _S ∩ forb = ∅_), so the authors clearly have a definition in mind.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'Z' as both set variable and placeholder in list
comments.quote: There are six valid backdoor adjustment sets _Z_ = _{Z_ 1 _, Z_ 2 _, Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 _, Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 _, Z_ 1 _Z_ 3 _, Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 _}_ .
comments.explanation: Here, the symbol _Z_ is used both as a bound variable ranging over adjustment sets (in general) and as the name of a specific set in the enumeration. The phrase '_Z_ = {Z₁, Z₂, Z₁Z₂, ...}' suggests that the entire collection is being assigned to _Z_, which contradicts earlier use where _Z_ denotes a single adjustment set. However, careful reading shows that this is likely a notational shorthand: the author means 'the valid adjustment sets are: Z₁, Z₂, Z₁Z₂, ...' and uses _Z_ loosely as a metavariable. This is common in informal exposition. The full paper context confirms that **Z** denotes the *set of all valid adjustment sets*, so this line is best read as 'valid sets include Z₁, Z₂, etc.' rather than a formal assignment. While potentially confusing at first glance, the meaning is recoverable and consistent with standard usage in causal literature.

comments.title: Venn diagram misalignment with Definition 1 of adjustment information
comments.quote: An optimal adjustment set **Z** must maximize the adjustment information _I_ **Z** ; _Y |X_ **S** _− IX_ ; **Z** _|_ **S** (blue and red hatched, respectively).
comments.explanation: The visual description in Figure 1C attributes the term _I_(**Z**; Y | X**S**) to the 'blue hatched' region and _I_(X; **Z** | **S**) to the 'red hatched' region, and claims that their difference (blue − red) should be maximized. However, this interpretation risks confusion: CMI is not directly additive in Venn diagrams unless Gaussian or binary, and mutual information regions do not subtract like set areas. More problematically, the diagram may mislead readers into thinking that _I_(X; **Z** | **S**) is to be *avoided* (since subtracted), while _I_(**Z**; Y | X**S**) is to be maximized. But in fact, both terms are necessary: high _I_(**Z**; Y | X**S**) means **Z** predicts Y after X is controlled (good for reducing outcome variance), while low _I_(X; **Z** | **S**) means X is not overly constrained by **Z** (preserves treatment variation). The Venn diagram, while illustrative, risks implying that minimizing _I_(X; **Z** | **S**) alone is the goal—even at the expense of **Z**'s predictive power—when both terms matter. However, the text correctly defines _J_**Z** as a *difference*, and the explanation in Section 2.1 supports the proper interpretation. So while the visualization is heuristic, it doesn't introduce a formal error.

comments.title: Missing justification for equating asymptotic variance with adjustment entropy in non-linear cases
comments.quote:
One can also further restrict the class to estimators whose (square-root of the) asymptotic variance can be expressed as

for a real-valued, strictly monotonously increasing function of the adjustment entropy. Minimizing the adjustment entropy is by Eq. (6) equivalent to maximizing the adjustment information.
comments.explanation: The adjustment entropy is implicitly defined as _H_(Y | X**ZS**) + _H_(X | **ZS**), and maximizing _J_**Z** is equivalent to minimizing this sum due to Equation (6): _J_**Z** = _H_(Y | X**S**) − _H_(Y | X**ZS**) − [_H_(X | **S**) − _H_(X | **ZS**)] = [_H_(X | **ZS**) − _H_(Y | X**ZS**)] + const. So maximizing _J_**Z** minimizes (_H_(Y | X**ZS**) − _H_(X | **ZS**)), but not the sum. The paper claims minimizing 'adjustment entropy' (interpreted as sum) is equivalent to maximizing _J_**Z**, but this is only true if one component dominates or if a weighted combination is used. In fact, from the Gaussian case later, where _H_(Y|X**ZS**) and _H_(X|**ZS**) are log-variances, the asymptotic variance of OLS depends on _σ^2_Y|X**ZS_ / _σ^2_X|**ZS_, whose log is precisely _H_(Y|X**ZS**) − _H_(X|**ZS**), which *is* directly related to _J_**Z_. So the correct quantity is the *difference* of entropies, not the sum. The phrase 'adjustment entropy' likely refers to this combined functional, but the term is misleading—it is not a standard entropy. This could confuse readers expecting 'entropy' to be additive. However, the logic in Lemma 2 and the Gaussian derivation remain valid, so the error is in labeling, not substance.

comments.title: Unjustified claim that OLS in linear models satisfies relation (9) without Gaussian noise
comments.quote: This relation is also the basis of the results for the causally sufficient case in Henckel et al. [2019] where it is shown that it holds more generally for causal linear models that do not require the noise terms to be Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the asymptotic variance of OLS depends only on conditional variances (hence, entropies) even for non-Gaussian linear models, so maximizing _J_**Z** minimizes asymptotic variance. However, for non-Gaussian errors, the asymptotic variance of OLS does depend on higher moments (kurtosis), not just variances. The standard asymptotic variance of _βˆ_ is (X'X)^{−1} X'ΩX (X'X)^{−1}, where Ω is the covariance of residuals. Under homoskedasticity and correct model specification, this reduces to σ² (X'X)^{−1}, and σ² is estimated consistently regardless of distribution. But in finite samples and even asymptotically under non-normality, the *precision* of σ² estimation depends on kurtosis, and hence the variance of _βˆ_ can depend on the full distribution. More critically, if the structural equations are linear but errors are non-Gaussian, the conditional entropies _H_(Y|X**ZS**) and _H_(X|**ZS**) still depend only on variances only if the distribution is elliptical (e.g., Gaussian). For non-Gaussian linear models, mutual information involves more than just log-variance. Therefore, equating adjustment information (defined via entropy) with estimator variance is only exact under Gaussianity. The reference to Henckel et al. [2019] likely relies on homoskedastic linear models where the *relative ordering* of variances across adjustment sets depends only on R² terms, which *are* functions of partial variances. So while the *optimal set* might still be the same under non-Gaussian linear models, the exact information-theoretic equivalence claimed in relation (9) does not hold. This overstates the generality of the result.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of adjustment information inequality in Lemma 1
comments.quote:
if and only if there is a Z ∈Z such that either there is no other Z[′] = Z ∈Z or for all other Z[′] = Z ∈Z and all distributions P consistent with G it holds that

then graphical optimality holds and Z is optimal implying J_Z ≥ J_Z′.
comments.explanation: The quoted passage introduces Lemma 1, which purports to provide a necessary and sufficient condition for graphical optimality via an inequality involving adjustment information. However, the inequalities (7) referenced here are not displayed in the passage—or even in the immediate vicinity in the provided text—which makes it impossible for the reader to verify what the claimed condition actually is. While it's plausible that (7) was defined earlier (e.g., in the full paper), in the passage, this creates a serious disconnect: the lemma is stated as if the condition is explicit, but the mathematical content is missing. This is not just a formatting issue; it breaks the logical flow and prevents a reader from assessing the validity of the argument. Although the intent is clear—to characterize optimality through information inequalities—the omission of the actual inequalities undermines the rigor at a critical juncture. Context from surrounding definitions (e.g., J_Z) helps, but not enough to reconstruct the exact condition. This is especially problematic because the paper positions Lemma 1 as foundational to its main result.

comments.title: Incorrect entropy expression for Gaussian variables
comments.quote: For Gaussians the entropies in (9) are given by H(Y|X ZS) = 2[1][+][1] 2[ln(2] [πσ] Y_[2] _|X ZS[)] and [ H][(][X|] [ZS] =] 1[1][Then] 2[+] 2[ln(2] [πσ] X_[2] _| ZS[)][ where] [ σ][(][·|·] displays the square-root of the conditional variance.]
comments.explanation: The expression for the differential entropy of a Gaussian variable is H(Y | X ZS) = (1/2) ln(2πeσ²), not (1/2) ln(2πσ²) as implied by the garbled text. The OCR appears to have corrupted the LaTeX significantly: '[1][+]' and '[1] 2[ln(]' suggest something like '1/2 ln', while '2[1]' might be '1/2'. Also, the lack of an 'e' in the logarithm is a critical mathematical error. Even assuming intended meaning, the standard formula includes 'e', so H = (1/2) ln(2πeσ²). The omitted 'e' would lead to an incorrect relationship between entropy and variance. This error occurs in the context of justifying the estimator class, which underpins Lemma 2 and the connection between J_Z and asymptotic variance. While the authors correctly relate entropy to conditional variance, the wrong coefficient would break this link. Given that this is central to the theoretical justification for Gaussian models, and no acknowledgment of the approximation is provided, this is a substantive technical flaw.

comments.title: Misleading claim about equivalence of maximizing J_Z and minimizing adjustment entropy
comments.quote: Minimizing the adjustment entropy is by Eq. (6) equivalent to maximizing the adjustment information.
comments.explanation: The paper defines J_Z = I_{Z;Y|XS} - I_{X;Z|S} and later refers to 'adjustment entropy' without defining it. The term appears only in the sentence claiming equivalence between minimizing 'adjustment entropy' and maximizing J_Z. Given context, the authors seem to imply that 'adjustment entropy' refers to a combination of H(Y|XZS) and H(X|ZS), since these appear in the entropy decomposition of the CMI terms. However, J_Z is a difference of CMIs, not directly a function of entropies in a way that makes its maximization equivalent to minimizing a single 'adjustment entropy' quantity. In fact, maximizing J_Z involves trading off increasing I_{Z;Y|XS} (which decreases H(Y|XZS)) and decreasing I_{X;Z|S} (which increases H(X|ZS)). So one cannot frame this as minimizing a single entropy term without further assumptions (e.g., fixed marginal distributions). The claim oversimplifies the trade-off and could mislead a reader into thinking the objective is unidimensional. While the high-level intuition aligns with entropy minimization in specific cases (e.g., fixed X distribution), the general equivalence is false. This overstates the theoretical grounding of the optimization objective.

comments.title: Underspecified 'valid collider path' definition
comments.quote: we call a collider path node set πW[i] for W∈Y M valid wrt. (X, Y, S) if for each path node C ∈ πW[i] both of the following conditions are fulfilled:
comments.explanation: The text states that two conditions are to be fulfilled for valid collider paths but does not list them in the provided passage. This is a critical omission: Definition 3 is incomplete. The subsequent sentence refers to '(2a) and (2b)', but these are not displayed. While the summary mentions that (1) C ∉ forb and (2) C ⊥⊥ X | vancs (from context), here it's unclear whether those are the actual conditions. Moreover, the definition of a 'valid' collider path hinges on these, and without them, the reader cannot assess whether the O-set construction is sound. The algorithm for the O-set (referenced later) depends on this definition, yet it is underspecified in a way that prevents verification or reproduction. This is not a formatting issue but a substantive gap in method description. The fact that the definition builds on vancs and forb shows some care, but omitting the actual conditions makes this portion of the method non-reproducible.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of valid collider path node conditions
comments.quote: If jointly (2a) and (2b) are not fulfilled, i.e. _C ∈/_ **vancs** and _C⊥⊥X |_ **vancs** , then the collider path stops before _C_ .
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence appears to clarify when a valid collider path terminates. However, the phrasing 'if jointly (2a) and (2b) are not fulfilled' is logically problematic because in Definition 3, both (2a) and (2b) must be fulfilled for validity — so if either fails, the condition fails. But the text rewrites this as 'C ∈/ vancs and C ⊥⊥ X | vancs', which suggests both must fail simultaneously for termination. This creates confusion: should the path stop if either condition fails, or only if both fail? From context and the use of 'and' in the negated form of an 'or' condition, it seems the author intends the former — that if either condition fails, the path stops. However, the sentence as written implies a joint failure is required, which is stronger than needed. This mischaracterization could mislead a careful reader about the path selection criterion, especially since later examples (like Example C) rely on correctly identifying N-nodes based on failure of just one condition. The core idea — pruning paths at nodes not in vancs or d-separated from X given vancs — is sound and aligns with standard graphical criteria, but the logical phrasing undermines clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistency in notation between C and CPC subsets
comments.quote: the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** _\_ **P** _⊆_ **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid.
comments.explanation: The notation **CPC** \ **P** is used here to denote the set of collider path nodes and their parents, excluding those already in **P**. However, earlier in Definition 4, **PC** = pa(**C**) \ (forb ∪ **S**) is defined, so **CPC** is not formally defined. From context, **CPC** likely means **C** ∪ **PC**, but this compound symbol has not been introduced. Moreover, using **CPC** \ **P** suggests a set difference, but **CPC** is interpreted as **C** ∪ **PC**, so **CPC** \ **P** would be (**C** \ **P**) ∪ (**PC** \ **P**). This relies on **C** ∩ **P** = ∅ and **PC** ∩ **P** = ∅, which may not always hold — descendants or parents could coincidentally be shared. While the author's intent appears to be minimizing only the added collider-related components (i.e., **C** and **PC** but not **P**), the notation is ambiguous and creates an unnecessary risk of misinterpretation. The text later refers to 'CPC \ P ⊆ O', which assumes the reader infers **CPC** as a union, but this should be formally specified or replaced with the correct expression.

comments.title: Missing justification for order-independence of minimization procedures
comments.quote: Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly emphasize order-independence for the construction of **O** in Algorithm C.1, due to the independence of collider path node addition criteria. However, when transitioning to **O**min and **O**Cmin, the claim that these are order-independent 'since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops' is insufficient. The mere fact that removal occurs post-loop does not guarantee that the result of minimization is unique — minimization over a set is order-independent only if the validity of subsets is monotonic and the space of valid subsets is closed under intersection or union, which is non-trivial in adjustment set minimization. The cited algorithms in van der Zander et al. [2019] do guarantee order-independence through specific greedy procedures, but no reference to such a property is made here for Algorithm C.2. Given that the numerical experiments depend on reproducibility, this underspecification is concerning. While the claim may be true under the structure of the **O**-set, it requires justification via graph-theoretic arguments (e.g., antichain structure of valid subsets), which is absent.

comments.title: Incorrect logical formulation in Theorem 2 condition for equality
comments.quote: We have J_O = J_vancs only if (1) O = vancs, or (2) O ⊆ vancs and X ⊥⊥ vancs \ O | OS.
comments.explanation: The theorem states a necessary condition for equality in adjustment information. However, the phrasing 'only if' suggests these are the only two possible scenarios under which J_O = J_vancs, but the structure of the adjustment information J_Z = I(Z; Y|XS) − I(X; Z|S) implies other possibilities — for example, if the additional variables in O \ vancs are conditionally independent of both Y given XS and X given S, their contribution could cancel out even if O is not a subset of vancs. The second condition assumes O ⊆ vancs, which may exclude such cases. More critically, if O properly contains vancs, but the extra variables add no mutual information with Y given XS and also do not increase I(X; Z|S), equality could still hold. The sufficiency of the given conditions is not in question, but the necessity (due to 'only if') is too strong. Context suggests the authors may mean 'if' rather than 'only if', which would make the statement safer. As written, the claim is potentially false and overreaching, which is problematic for a theoretical result that applies to any graph.

comments.title: Unclear status of 'AdjustXmin' and potential conflation with other sets
comments.quote: Based on the idea in **O** Cmin, in the numerical experiments we also consider AdjustXmin, where only Adjust \ pa(Y M) is minimized and pa(Y M) is always included.
comments.explanation: The description of AdjustXmin is underspecified. The text says only ``Adjust \ pa(Y M) is minimized'', but it does not specify what the goal of minimization is — is it validity? minimal cardinality? robustness? Without referencing an algorithm or criterion, it is unclear how AdjustXmin differs from Adjustmin or O_Cmin in practice. Moreover, since Adjust = vancs is generally larger than O, and pa(Y M) is part of both, AdjustXmin could end up being similar to Omin but without collider additions — but this is not stated. The procedural vagueness makes it difficult to interpret the experimental comparison involving AdjustXmin, especially when extending theoretical claims. For reproducibility and clarity, this modification should be formally defined or tied to a specific algorithm, rather than being introduced parenthetically in the method description.

comments.title: Misleading implication about OLS estimator coverage in Assumptions 2
comments.quote: The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation. ... At present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case.
comments.explanation: While this passage is not in the checked segment, its context is crucial: the current text claims Assumptions 2 apply to a wide estimator class, but later explicitly restricts theoretical verification to linear models and OLS. In the checked passage, however, the discussion of entropy and OLS variance assumes Gaussianity. The issue arises when interpreting Lemma 2: it claims 'if and only if' equivalence between J_Z ≥ J_Z′ and asymptotic variance under Assumptions 2. But this equivalence holds strictly only under correct model specification and the Gaussian linear model assumption, as acknowledged later. Presenting it as general for any estimator satisfying (9) — without proving that (9) holds beyond OLS with Gaussian errors — overstates the generality. Readers might infer that the result applies nonparametrically or for non-Gaussian errors, but this is not the case. The field standard (e.g., in HPM19) is to clearly delimit the estimator class. This is a moderate overstatement that could mislead applied researchers about the method's scope.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing in Theorem 3 condition for N-nodes
comments.quote: Denote the set of N-nodes by **N** = _sp_ ( _Y_ **MC** ) _\_ ( **forbOS** ) . Finally, given an N ∈_ **N** _and a collider path N ↔· · · ↔C↔· · · ↔W (including N ↔W ) for C ∈_ **C** _and W ∈ Y_ **M** _(indexed by i) with the collider path nodes denoted by πi[N][(excluding][ N][and][ W][), denote by]_ **[ O]** _π_ i[N][=] **[ O]**[(] _[X, Y,]_ **[ S]** _[′]_[=] **[ S]** _[Nπ] i[N]_[)] _[ the O-set for the causal effect] of X on Y given_ **S** _[′]_ = **S** _∪{N } ∪ πi[N][.
comments.explanation: The indexing of the collider path, particularly the expression 'πi[N][(excluding][ N][and][ W][)', is confusing upon first reading. It suggests that πi[N] denotes the internal nodes of the i-th collider path from N to some W ∈ Y**M**, but the notation 'πi[N]' appears to treat N as a subscript rather than the starting node of the path—this could be an OCR misread of a more conventional 'π_i(N)' or 'π^{(i)}(N)'. Later in the statement of Theorem 3, the notation **O**_π_iN is used (e.g., in Example E: 'Cond. (II) is not fulfilled for E = Z₁'), which seems to refer to a modified O-set under an expanded conditioning set including N and path nodes. However, the precise definition of **S**′ = **S** ∪ {N} ∪ π_i[N] is problematic: is π_i[N] meant to be the internal collider path nodes along the i-th path from N to W? And is the union over all such paths? The construction is essential to Condition (I), which checks whether **O**_π_iN blocks all non-causal paths. But without clarification on how many paths are considered (all? any?), whether W must be in **M** or just Y, and whether the path must be entirely within **C**, the reproducibility and logical clarity of Theorem 3 is compromised. The surrounding discussion in Examples F and G implies the construction, but a reader would struggle to implement or verify Condition (I) algorithmically without further specification.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of O-set in text vs. algorithmic implication
comments.quote: **O** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 in Example E; Definition 4 states **O** = **P** ∪ **C** ∪ **PC**
comments.explanation: In Example E, the graph (as referenced from SSR20 and HPM19) contains _X_, _Y_, _Z_1 → _X_, _Z_2 ↔ _Y_, and possibly _Z_1 ↔ _Z_2. The text states **O** = _Z_1Z_2. According to Definition 4, **P** = pa(Y**M**) \ **forb**; assuming no mediators, **P** = ∅ since _Y_ has no parents. **C** is the set of valid collider path nodes from Y**M**; since _Z_2 ↔ _Y_, and if _Z_2 ∈ **vancs**, then _Z_2 ∈ **C**. **PC** = pa(**C**) = pa(_Z_2). If _Z_1 → _Z_2, then _Z_1 ∈ **PC**, so **O** = _Z_1Z_2. But if the edge is _Z_1 ↔ _Z_2_, is _Z_1 considered a parent of _Z_2? In ADMGs, spouses are connected by ↔, not →, so pa(_Z_2) would not include _Z_1 in that case. Yet the paper seems to treat bidirected adjacency as sufficient for inclusion in **PC**, which contradicts the definition **PC** = pa(**C**), since parents are only the directed in-neighbors. This implies either (1) an OCR or notational error in treating 'pa' too broadly, or (2) a real definition gap. The algorithm (C.1) must resolve this—likely by including spouses or adjacency in **PC** when colliders are involved. But without clarifying whether 'pa' in **PC** is meant in the strict DAG sense or extended to spouse parents in the mixed graph, the definition is ambiguous. This matters because **PC** determines inclusion of confounders like _Z_1 in **O**.

comments.title: Misleading claim about Example G's optimality relying solely on Z₂ ∈ vancs
comments.quote: Example G is optimal since there are no N-nodes and Z₂ ∈ vancs.
comments.explanation: The passage asserts that Example G is optimal 'since there are no N-nodes and Z₂ ∈ vancs'. However, according to Theorem 3, both Condition (I) and (II) must be satisfied. The presence of **S** changes **vancs**, possibly including both _Z_1 and _Z_2. But Condition (II) requires that for any E ∈ **O** \ **P** with an open path to X given **SO** \ {E}, there exists a link or collider path to Y**M** through **C** with colliders in **vancs**. In Example G, if **O** = _Z_1Z_2, and _Z_1 ∈ **PC**, then E = _Z_1 must be checked: is there an open path from _Z_1 to X given **SO** \ {_Z_1}? If _Z_1 → X directly, and no other paths, then conditioning on _Z_2 may not block that path. But if _Z_1 ⊥⊥ X | **SO** \ {_Z_1}, then Condition (II) may be vacuously satisfied. However, the text reduces the justification to 'no N-nodes and Z₂ ∈ vancs', which is insufficient to conclude Condition (II) holds. N-nodes relate to Condition (I), but Condition (II) is independent. Hence, the justification is underspecified and could mislead a reader into thinking N-node absence and vancs membership are jointly sufficient, when in fact both conditions are separate and must be verified independently. This is a simplification beyond what the theorem provides.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'graphically optimal' in Examples E and F
comments.quote: Example E. The example in Fig. 2E ... is not graphically optimal. ... a superior adjustment set depends on how strong the link Z₁→X vs. Z₂↔Y is. ... Example F. The example in Fig. 2F is also not graphically optimal.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'not graphically optimal' is used correctly—meaning that no single adjustment set uniformly minimizes variance across all distributions consistent with the graph. The explanation involving dependence on link strengths is standard and aligns with Definition 2. However, the transition from Example E to Example F shifts without clarification: in F, **O** = ∅, and _Z_₂ is an N-node. The paper claims **O** is not optimal because for some distributions, **O**_π_iN ≔ **O**(X,Y,{S,Z₂}) = _Z_₁Z₂ yields higher J. But **O**(X,Y,{S,Z₂}) is not a competitor adjustment set under the original **S**—it's defined under a different conditioning. The argument relies on constructing a counterexample distribution under an augmented setting, which is valid, but the leap is not clearly justified. A careful reader might question whether comparing adjustment information under different **S** is legitimate. However, the construction in Theorem 3's Condition (I) explicitly uses **O**_π_iN with expanded **S**′, so the logic is internally consistent. Thus, while initially puzzling, the argument is resolved by the formalism in Theorem 3. The issue is more one of pedagogical clarity than a real error.

comments.title: Incorrect information-theoretic derivation in Example E
comments.quote: since JZ₁Z₂ = J∅ + IZ₂;Y|XZ₁ − IX;Z₁, a superior adjustment set depends on how strong the link Z₁→X vs. Z₂↔Y is.
comments.explanation: The equation J_{Z₁Z₂} = J_∅ + I_{Z₂;Y|XZ₁} − I_{X;Z₁} appears in the example. Let's verify it. By Definition 1: J_**Z** = I(**Z**;Y|XS) − I(X;**Z**|S). Then: J_{Z₁Z₂} = I(Z₁Z₂;Y|XS) − I(X;Z₁Z₂|S) = [I(Z₁;Y|XS) + I(Z₂;Y|XZ₁S)] − [I(X;Z₁|S) + I(X;Z₂|Z₁S)]. Assuming S = ∅, and Z₁ ⊥⊥ Y|X (from the example), then I(Z₁;Y|X) = 0. Also, assuming X ⊥⊥ Z₂|Z₁, then I(X;Z₂|Z₁) = 0. So J_{Z₁Z₂} = I(Z₂;Y|XZ₁) − I(X;Z₁). Meanwhile, J_∅ = 0 − 0 = 0. So J_{Z₁Z₂} = I(Z₂;Y|XZ₁) − I(X;Z₁) = J_∅ + I(Z₂;Y|XZ₁) − I(X;Z₁). This derivation is correct under the stated independences. Initially, one might think I(X;Z₁) should be conditioned on S, but S=∅. The claim is valid and consistent with the information-theoretic framework. No error here after verification.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about O-set's variance compared to Adjust-set
comments.quote: Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement seems to suggest a universal dominance of the **O**-set over the Adjust-set in terms of variance, which would be a strong claim. However, earlier in the abstract and introduction, the author states that the **O**-set has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set. Given Lemma 2, which links higher adjustment information to lower asymptotic variance under Assumptions 2, this would imply lower or equal variance — aligning with the claim. But the issue arises in the passage under review: in non-optimal settings, the **O**-set is said to perform worse than **O**Cmin and AdjustXmin in about half the cases. While that doesn't directly contradict **O** outperforming Adjust, the later statement in the Discussion generalizes the variance advantage across *all* graphs and *any* validity status, which goes beyond what is supported by the evidence. The numerical results show **O** often underperforms its *minimized variants*, suggesting it doesn't universally dominate *all* other adjustment sets in variance, even if it may dominate Adjust. The claim here overreaches by asserting a strict variance ordering relative to Adjust without qualification by estimator type or sample size, despite the experiments showing that AdjustXmin sometimes beats **O**. Thus, while **O** may dominate Adjust in *adjustment information* (as supported), the direct translation to *variance dominance* across all graphs and distributions — especially when **O** has higher cardinality and hurts finite-sample performance — is not fully justified, particularly outside the class of estimators satisfying relation (9).

comments.title: Misstatement of O-set's optimality in non-optimal graphical settings
comments.quote: Note that in cases where graphical optimality does not hold, there will still be distributions for which the **O** -set has maximal adjustment information.
comments.explanation: This claim appears reasonable at first: graphical optimality fails when no single set is optimal across *all* distributions, but for *some* distributions, **O** might still be best. However, the issue lies in the implication that **O** is even a candidate for such optimality. The construction of **O** is motivated as the *graphical* optimal set when conditions of Theorem 3 hold. When they fail, the paper acknowledges that optimality depends on distributional strengths (e.g., edge coefficients). But there is no proof or argument that **O**, specifically, is the set most likely to achieve maximal adjustment information when graphical optimality fails. It could be that for certain distributions, some other set **Z** ∉ {**O**, Adjust, ...} maximizes _J_**Z**, and **O** may not even be competitive. The statement assumes **O** is a natural maximizer of _J_**Z** even without graphical support, but the information-theoretic derivation of **O** is based on structural constraints, not distributional tuning. While it may *sometimes* happen, asserting it as a general property risks overstatement. The authors correctly note that **O** is valid iff any set is, and that it has high adjustment information, but they don’t establish that it is the *maximizer* across distributions when graphical conditions fail. This is a subtle but important distinction: being valid and informative ≠ being maximal for some P.

comments.title: Cardinality-variance trade-off mischaracterized without estimator context
comments.quote: Cardinality is slightly higher for **O** compared to all other sets. In Fig. S7 we further differentiate the results by the cardinality of the **O** -set and find that for small cardinalities (up to 4) the **O** -set has the lowest variance in a majority of cases, but for higher cardinalities either **O** Cmin or again **O** have the lowest variance (slightly beating AdjustXmin).
comments.explanation: The passage implies that **O** performs well even at high cardinalities, which appears to downplay the cost of high-dimensional adjustment. However, this finding is contradicted later when discussing small sample sizes (n=30), where **O**Cmin outperforms **O** due to cardinality. The issue is not a contradiction *per se*, but the lack of integration between these observations. The claim that **O** sometimes outperforms **O**Cmin at high cardinalities — especially for non-parametric estimators like MLP and DML — is surprising given finite-sample variance inflation from overadjustment. While Fig. S7 may show this, the explanation is underspecified.
